speaker: interviewer
text: You arrived, as you indicated, IN Haifa in 1947, I believe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, just before the declaration of the United Nations about structuring of the Jewish state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you briefly describe your life, starting from Haifa, to date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's relatively simple. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a wandering Jews story. We-- we wandered from place to place. We came to Haifa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Eventually, my stepmother bought a house. She-- she remarried in the second-- let's make-- let's do it straight. In November, 1947, the United Nation declared, on the so-called partition and structuring of the Jewish state. The Arabs invaded, not accepting that decision-- the decision of the United Nations. We were, at the time, living and being supported by a-- a family member, a family of my stepmother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They courted. But the war [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] forced her to make a decision. If he would not be married, he would not be able to support her anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they married, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bought a house in Kiryat Motskin, which is a suburb of Haifa. And I went to the school there. I finished-- I was-- when I came there, I was placed one grade lower than I should have.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was very little support for people like me in that school. The big waves-- that was before the War of Independence. The big waves of the immigrants didn't come yet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there was no infrastructure to support people who don't know Hebrew. I had some basic in Heb-- basis in Hebrew. I know-- I knew how to read. I knew the-- the shape of the characters. But I didn't know Hebrew. So I was-- my-- my stepmother arranged for a private tutor. I was taught Hebrew, eventually moved, reasonably quickly, to my regular grade. And apart from deficiency in Hebrew grammar, I was quite a good-- good student. Finished high school in Kiryat Motskin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the time, obviously, Israel was already a nation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I finished high school in 1955. Israel was a nation. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] applied and was accepted to what's called [SPEAKING HEBREW], or, in English, the academic reserve. It's a special-- it's similar to ROTC in this country. It is a very, very selective-- very strict, selective process. A small number are accepted. People are ordered-- rank ordered. Everybody's given a number and the-- at the time, and the army would say we want X. And everybody whose number less than X would be in. In my case, the-- the X was under 20. And I was number 5, so I was very com-- comfortably in. I finished a degree in the Technion. I was accepted to the Technion. Finished a degree at the Technion, undergraduate degree in 1959 in physics, general physics. And then I had to go to the army. A very, very small number of us were offered the opportunity, instead of going to the army, to spend the army service in the research establishment of the Israeli Ministry of Defense. They needed people with certain skills. They couldn't get them. The army was ready to give them. I went through a set of exams. I was selected. I went there. I had to sign up for lengthier stay in the armed forces, but the choice of running up and down hills and shooting holes in an air, relative to being a research member of a very elite and hush-hush part of the Israeli Ministry of Defense-- at that time, it was not even known to exist-- It was something I couldn't forgo. I became a member of that group, which, later, was known as-- now, it's-- it's in the open and known as the Armament Authority-- Armament Development Authority. In Hebrew, it was called RAFAEL, which is an acronym of [HEBREW], which is [INAUDIBLE] the Armament Development Authority. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I rose through the ranks quite quickly. And in parallel, because I was based around Haifa, I went to graduate school, finished a master's degree, part time, in astrophysics, finished a doctoral degree, part time, in physics, while working. By 1967, I had a doctoral degree, fighting a little war in the meantime. I was a very integral part of that war, being where I was. It's not something I am going to discuss in detail, obviously, but I-- I-- I had a major part in what was happening there. And I was, therefore, not in the armed forces. I was stationed there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was an interesting episode. Before the Six Day War, I was there. I was living, essentially, from coffee. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would come home for three, four hours, and go back. And that was all I slept, 3-4 hours. And eventually, I-- I start to be busy. I went to a doctor on base. And he told me, jokingly, that there's nothing for me to worry. In a week, I'll be dead anyway, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, what do you mean? I said, I needed to block this. He said, the blood is so full of caffeine that I should stop drinking and I'll have caffeine for the rest of my life. I should stop, really, to drink. I told him I cannot drink. He said, well, you don't need-- don't need caffeine. You're full of caffeine. I said, I need the coffee psychologically. So he said, what do you put in the coffee? I said, what do you mean? What every sane person puts, sugar and milk. He said, stop the sugar, stop the milk. I said, how can you drink black coffee? He said, drink black coffee. So I had this cup the whole day. I couldn't make myself to drink. I survived. Now, I drink black coffee. But an episode like that, I think-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I did what I did, all my career, throughout those years, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I felt the duty to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to be part of the armed forces. I wanted to be part of the defense so this would not happen again. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I believe I did my share. I have a picture here with Ben-Gurion, who was the architect of the State of Israel, in a sense. One can play with yes or no. It's a political discussion. I was very successful. I put all my life in it. I sacrificed everything else. And I'm very proud. I'm very pleased that I did it. I-- I had a major role in projects which shaped, I believe, the-- which shaped the armed forces, and which contributed to the welfare of the Jewish people. After a while, I-- I decided that I-- I was sent to this country in various ways. After a while, I decided that I don't know enough. I have to go back to learn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Ministry of Defense decided that I know enough. They didn't want me to go to learn. I resigned. I was offered a position here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you resign?

speaker: interviewee
text: I resigned in 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I was married. I had two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of your wife and children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I-- I married Dalia-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] her maiden name is Bogler-- in 1960. It was a military marriage because, at the time, I was still in the military. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have three children-- Ophir and Tally, both born in Israel, and Gony, born in this country. We all live, now, here in-- in the Washington area. Tally was just married a couple of months ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came-- Dalia, Ophir, and Tally in 1970. We stayed a couple of years. Then we went back for two years. I worked for IBM, and I was part of the air force. Because once I left-- once I left the Ministry of Defense, I was put in the reserves. I was put in the air force.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Israeli Air Force?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Israeli Air Force. And I was put in quite a high position in, again, one of the not publicized-- not very highly publicized-- units of the air force, and stayed there in reserves [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for two years, on and off, being called back and working for them more than typical reserve officer would. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Eventually, we came to this country because I wanted to-- to switch to academia. And the Israeli universities [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] could not accept me-- would not accept me on the low level I want-- I was ready to go, because I should have gone on the high level. But they said, you know, how can we accept you on this low level. It was-- it left a certain layer of bitterness, but not for the country, God forbid, but for that particular episode with that particular institution. And at the time when I was discussing with them what I can and cannot do, somebody from the United States heard about it-- I was quite well-known at the time-- and he offered me a position I just could not refuse. He offered me a tenured position with reasonable salary in a good university. I just couldn't refuse it. So we all immigrated to this country in 1975, and we are here since then. My children finished--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what university did you start out with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I joined the University-- the State University of New York in Buffalo. I was later offered the position of a chair in the University of Michigan. If you are offered a position of a chair at the University of Michigan, you don't say no. It's a-- it's a superb university. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually, we wandered from there to the University of Michigan, stayed there six years. Then my wife was offered a position in Syracuse, and I followed her to Syracuse. And then I was offered a position of a dean here. And we came here in 1992 to the George Washington University. And you've been there ever since?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm there since--

speaker: interviewer
text: As the dean?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a dean of the School of Engineering and Applied Science. And possibly we'll stay a couple of more years and then go back to the faculty. And eventually, retire. I hope to retire around the age of 70, if I'll be healthy till then. It's 11 years from now. A couple of years more dean, a couple of year more faculty, and then a couple more years to phase out, if-- god willing-- everything will be all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you think back over all of your experiences, Holocaust, what are the lessons to be learned in your judgment?

speaker: interviewee
text: The lesson we learn is that we don't learn. This is not original statement by me. Somebody said, the only thing we learn from the history is that we don't learn. I went back. The hatred is still there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The only-- only thing I learned is that nobody will help you when you are in time of need except those people close to you. That's the reason I worked so hard for the security of Israel, and that's the reason I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] believe so hard in the Jewish nation, the Jewish people. Nobody helps you. Nobody. We didn't have anybody. Nor do have the people in Biafra, when they were killed, or in Bangladesh, or now in Africa, or the Armenians, or any other of the genocides which happened. I don't believe in humanity very much. Humanity didn't prove itself. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I just came from a totally closed and shocked. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I'm very disillusioned. And I don't think that anybody who lived from it can be optimistic. And I-- I really optimistic in nature. I always hoped. I always believed in people. And always-- this is now going back to Slovakia-- yes, there are points of light. But unless something dramatic happens, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we are on our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Frieder, I want to thank you so much for this interview. This terminates the interview. And we will-- I believe we will show some pictures in a short while. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my family, which I am very happy with, and which is the compensation for the pain I had. My wife, Dalia. We married in 1960. My son, Ophir, my daughter, Tally, and my other daughter, Gony. We introduced them by the order of their birth. So he's the oldest, Tally's the second, Gony is the youngest. If they have anything to say, they will say it, but we will do it in the reverse order, starting with Gony, and then Tally, and then Ophir, and then Dalia, if she wants to say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I think we've benefited from having a father who is a survivor, because we have all learned from a very young age, even though my mother has done much of the teaching, about the Holocaust. And it was never an issue of whether we were too young to learn about the Holocaust, or whether it would be too scary for us. It was always presented. Or it was just a presence in our lives, I think. I don't know if my siblings agree with me. And I remember, when I was nine, my dad wanted me to see a movie called And I didn't want to see it. My brother persuaded me to see it. And I remember thinking, in the movie, that I had already seen many of the images. And when I look back on that, I think that they both did a very good job educating me about what has happened, and directing me so that it wouldn't stifle my growth, but that it would add. That's it. So it's your turn.

speaker: interviewee
text: About the Holocaust, it's very much shaped who we are as people. The experiences my father went through affected everything surrounding our lives. My dad, luckily, has been able to approach everything with a sense of humor. Considering the horrific things that he went through, he still approaches life with a sense of humor and laughter. But it's helped us put things in perspective. You look at your daily experiences, and you see, in respect to what he has gone through, really what we're going through is nothing in respect to it. And he's helped us value our occurrences in life and move on every day with our own problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish to echo what my two sisters have said, but I wish to take it from a different perspective in this regard. I welcome, wholeheartedly, the efforts of a videotaping such cont-- such discussions and commentary that has been taking place, recently, in the last several years. I recall from a-- as being the eldest of the two of them, I have probably the strongest memories of the days gone by when, in the past, my father would never discuss, or would you never hear commentary about what happened in the past. And I think this is common for all the survivors, they-- from the ones who were very young at the time to the ones were somewhat older and are still around. The past several years, we've seen changes in my father and in this community that talks, that realizes they're getting older. And if we don't document these kind of situations, see the horrors that are taking place still today in many different nations, this would be unfortunately repeated. I just came back from a tour of Germany. I was there for professional reasons. And I saw that the German population is no different today, just a different target that we have now, because there are no Jews there. So now it's the Turks that are being attacked. It is very clear that, unless the world pays attention to what's going on, we're going to have the same kind of situations. Fortunately, people like my dad are now starting to speak out about what happened in the past. And fortunately, people like you are documenting it, so that the world, hopefully, will not repeat the same horrors that they have done in the past.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in conclusion, I just want to thank you for-- for your efforts, and I'm glad to see that people like my father, and my mother, and my sister are more attuned and are now talking about it. And the United States, for example, has a museum to record it. I just wish the rest of the world would have one, or to pay attention to it, unlike what's going on now in places like Germany and their versions of the Holocaust memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know to what extent what I'm going to say is a repetition, but Gideon and I started to date when we were in high school. So basically, I knew him since I was 14 and 1/2. And we dated for five years before we got married. And I would say that, all through that period, I never knew what happened in his past. And bits and pieces, from other people, I learned, and I put together a story that had very little base in reality because it was all rumors and hearsays and not very accurate. And I would tend to say that, until late in the '80s, I really didn't know the entire story as it was, and the truth behind it. It's very true that we knew that he was a Holocaust survivor, but we didn't know details, and we didn't know to what extreme he went through. In fact, when I had a phone call years ago from the person who basically saved his life-- Mr. Henry Herzog-- I said that, well, I know that you know my husband, because that's how he introduced himself. But I know my husband doesn't want to talk about the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't know if he would like to speak to you. And he was very upset by it, and he hung up on me. And in fact, that man published his book, and he stated that conversation that I had with him, as the wife refused to make contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was, indeed, the truth. He never wanted to speak about it until his uncle published his book in which I wanted to know a lot of personal information that was, unfortunately, omitted because of publication and editing. I believe that the Holocaust did have a very strong impact on our life, primarily because my husband grew as an orphan. And having no family surrounding, he really had lacked some sense of the importance of having a family around his life. And I was the one that always pushed for extending connections with relatives, with aunts and uncles, because he was very, I would say, deaf for any emotions to extend any relationship with any of his relatives that survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think it had a very strong effect on our family, because I felt I was the anchor in basically putting a family together and making us united. And maybe I overdid it, but I hope my kids will not think about it as such. But I think, basically, that's what my personal feeling is that I had a-- a man growing up with a sense of being a loner, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] feeling that he has to provide and survive. And that was the one motive that drove him further on in our life since. So these are the two aspects that I wanted just to add.

speaker: interviewee
text: Want to add anything? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Thank you very much. This is a picture myself in 1941. Remember, on the tape, I described the life in Nove Mesto. Here's the car I-- I referred to and the string, which I was pulled in, because I was just too short, at the time, to reach the pedals. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Picture from the same time. You see the same dress as-- as the other picture with my sister, Gita, from 1941. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] The picture should be from the-- from the garden of the villa, or from some other garden in Nove Mesto. It is an older picture showing me, I believe with my sister, but I'm not sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The picture after the war, age seven or eight. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After-- sometime after I was taken back from Buly, and possibly recovered a bit of my weight. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my father, [INAUDIBLE] Armin Frieder, close to the time of his death in 1946. He was around 35 years old at the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You can see now the card of condolence, the card which we sent back to the people who offered condolences. And you see the date of July, 1946, in Bratislava.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Picture of myself as a baby. It let me know that all the picture we have seen up to now, the sequence were brought by-- by Ophir and Tally from my Aunt Mansi, which was my maternal aunt, the sister of my mother. She died in 1994. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Picture with my mother, early '40s, from the same series from Aunt Mansi, which I have now to correct, 1990-1992. Note on myself the type of socks worn by children, to which I referred to in my story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Picture in the garden in Nove Mesto, myself and my sister. Possibly again, early-- early '40s.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Why not early '40s?

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she was--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Aunt Mansi, which was referred to as the source of these pictures, and my maternal grandmother. My maternal grandmother died somewhere in the '60s, so this would be an old picture. A picture of my father and mother. Note that she's covering her hair, as Jewish women of that era would do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what year it is, but it has to be well before '45. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the house in Buly where I spent the war. The electric box is obviously new. That's it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just a second. Why don't you start-- This is the house next to the house in Buly where I stayed, where the people were executed. You see there's a plaque on the house, which you can see more clearly now. It says that, in this place, people were executed. That's it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Picture of my father in the display in the Jewish Museum, part of the National Museum of Slovakia, describing the working of the Working Group, which saved Jews during the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a collage made my-- by Dalia, which has the picture of Anna, the daughter of the people with whom I stayed. Beneath it is the letter-- copy of the letter, which I sent to her, which she kept all these years, in which I thank them for everything. And to the left of it, there is a-- an embroidery that she made and sent us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] A drawing of ink on paper made by Gony. It was a cover for the Holocaust Memorial Day in Brandeis University, 1990, description of the type of heritage my children got, both pain and hope. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the original parchment, handwritten invitation and-- and appointment of my father as a rabbi in Nove Mesto. He was installed in January, 1938. These are calligraphy produced by my wife, Dalia, as a birthday present for me. It's an excerpt from a sermon of my father in 1943, in the Day of Atonement, in which he quoted from the high priest's ceremony, in which he said, quote, 'We must learn our lesson and know that if, God forbid, danger threatens us again, we must not sit in our homes and wait like sheep to be taken to the slaughter, lest our homes becomes graves.'
